Let me explore the codebase to understand the structure and find the relevant code.

Now I understand the structure. The error message in the failure log is coming from inside the GitHub Actions runner container itself — it's the runner's `gh-aw` (Actions Workflow) firewall/proxy system trying to pull images and failing because Docker isn't available at `/var/run/docker.sock`. This is the socket that `ghr` mounts into the runner container.

Let me look at how the socket mount is handled for macOS hosts:

Now I clearly see the issue. In `startDocker` (line 334-340 of `docker.go`), the socket mount (`sockFlags`) is **only added when `h.OS == "linux"`**. When the host OS is `darwin` (macOS), no socket mount is passed to the runner container, so the runner container can't find Docker at `/var/run/docker.sock`.

The same logic applies to `windows` too (no socket flags set for Windows hosts running docker-mode runners).

Let me check the macOS Docker socket path situation and see how Docker Desktop/OrbStack expose the socket on macOS:
Now I have a clear picture of the bug. Let me look at the `startDocker` function again and the macOS Docker socket path:
Now I have a complete understanding of the problem. Let me check the validation code to understand `docker_socket` constraint:
Now I have everything I need. Let me check what Docker socket paths macOS uses:
Now I have a crystal-clear understanding of the bug and scope. Let me also check how `docker_socket` is validated (only Linux currently), and look at the doctor code for macOS and Windows:

Now I have all the information needed to create the plan. Let me summarize the root cause and the full fix:

**Root Cause:** In `startDocker()` (line 334-340 of `docker.go`), `sockFlags` (the `-v socket:/var/run/docker.sock` mount) is only set when `h.OS == "linux"`. For macOS hosts (`darwin`) and Windows hosts running docker-mode runners, no socket is mounted into the container. The runner container therefore can't find Docker at `/var/run/docker.sock`, which causes the `gh-aw-firewall` and other Docker-dependent tooling inside the job to fail.

Additionally:
- `docker_socket` config field is validated to be Linux-only (needs to allow darwin too)
- `checkLinuxDockerSocket` in doctor is Linux-only (no socket check for darwin)
- On macOS, the socket path needs to be detected (Docker Desktop uses `/var/run/docker.sock` symlinked, OrbStack uses `~/.orbstack/run/docker.sock`, Colima uses `~/.colima/default/docker.sock`)
- Windows Docker Desktop exposes the daemon via a named pipe (`//./pipe/docker_engine`), not a socket — a Linux container can't mount that, so this is a different issue on Windows

input.description: Check macOS docs for docker_socket mention

content.text:
Wait — the Windows docs say "Docker-mode Linux containers include a bind mount of `/var/run/docker.sock`" but looking at the code, `sockFlags` is never set for Windows either! This is a documentation bug plus code bug.

For Windows, when Docker Desktop is in Linux containers mode, the socket is exposed at `/var/run/docker.sock` inside WSL2/Hyper-V, but when building a `docker run` command sent via PowerShell SSH, Docker Desktop on Windows actually mounts it automatically... Let me check if Docker Desktop on Windows exposes a named pipe vs socket mount:

Now I have a complete picture. The Windows docs say "bind mount of `/var/run/docker.sock`" in the linux-on-windows guide but the code doesn't do it! For macOS the docs say "not supported" but this is the bug causing the failure.

Let me understand the macOS socket path situation: On macOS, Docker Desktop/OrbStack/Colima expose the socket at `/var/run/docker.sock` (via symlink or directly). When you run `docker run -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`, on macOS, this works because the Docker CLI on the Mac already translates this to the VM path.

For **Windows**, Docker Desktop doesn't expose a Unix socket on the Windows host filesystem that can be bind-mounted. Instead, it exposes a named pipe `//./pipe/docker_engine`. However, Linux containers in Docker Desktop automatically have the Docker socket at `/var/run/docker.sock` inside the Hyper-V VM. When you run `docker run -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` on Windows (Linux containers mode), Docker does interpret this correctly — it maps the VM-internal socket.

Fix by extending socket mount logic to darwin and windows hosts, and update validation/doctor accordingly.

content.text: The file ends abruptly after the sentence about `docker_socket`. I need to add the example and complete the guide:

input.new_string:
**Docker socket permissions:** macOS runtimes (Docker Desktop, OrbStack, Colima) expose a VM-internal socket that is accessible to all processes — there is no host `docker` group GID issue. ghr skips `--group-add` on macOS hosts. ghr bind-mounts the Docker socket into the container at `/var/run/docker.sock` (the same default path as on Linux) so jobs can reach the Docker daemon. By default ghr uses `/var/run/docker.sock`; if your runtime uses a non-standard path (e.g. Colima with a named profile), set `docker_socket` in your host config:

```yaml
hosts:
  mac-mini:
    addr: user@192.168.1.50
    os: darwin
    arch: arm64
    docker_socket: /Users/me/.colima/myprofile/docker.sock  # optional; omit for Docker Desktop/OrbStack/default Colima
```

```yaml
hosts:
  mac-mini:
    addr: user@192.168.1.50
    os: darwin
    arch: arm64

runners:
  - name: myapp-mac-native
    repo: owner/repo
    host: mac-mini
    labels: [self-hosted, macOS, ARM64]

  - name: myapp-linux-on-mac
    repo: owner/repo
    host: mac-mini
    mode: docker
    labels: [self-hosted, Linux, ARM64]
```
input.old_string:

By default ghr uses `/var/run/docker.sock`; if your runtime uses a non-standard path (e.g. Colima with a named profile), set `docker_socket` in your host config:

```yaml

The `docker` CLI must work in the **same** environment as your SSH session (user, `PATH`, and Docker socket). Start the Docker engine (app or `colima start`, etc.) before relying on docker mode. **Docker socket permissions:** macOS Docker Desktop, OrbStack, and Colima use a Unix socket inside the VM that is accessible to all processes; ghr does not need `--group-add` on macOS and skips the GID detection step. ghr bind-mounts the socket at `/var/run/docker.sock` inside the container (matching the Linux default). Start the Docker engine (app or `colima start`, etc.) before relying on docker mode. **Docker socket permissions:** macOS Docker Desktop, OrbStack, and Colima use a Unix socket inside the VM that is accessible to all processes; ghr does not need `--group-add` on macOS and skips the GID detection step.

content.text: Now update the "Docker socket permissions (Linux)" section title and content to cover macOS too:

input.new_string:
### Docker socket permissions (Linux/macOS)

When `ghr` starts a docker-mode runner container on Linux or macOS, it bind-mounts the Docker socket into the container at `/var/run/docker.sock` so jobs can reach the Docker daemon. On **Linux**, the **`runner` user inside the container** (uid 1001) must also have permission to use the socket — ghr handles this automatically by:

1. Querying the socket's owning GID on the host with `stat -c '%g' /var/run/docker.sock`.
2. Passing `--group-add <GID>` to `docker run` so the container's runner user becomes a member of that group.
3. Running a pre-flight `test -S` check before starting the container to catch missing or mis-configured sockets early.

On **macOS** (Docker Desktop, OrbStack, Colima), the socket is accessible to all processes inside the VM — there is no docker group GID mismatch. ghr skips the `stat` query and `--group-add` on macOS hosts; only the bind-mount is applied.
input.old_string:
### Docker socket permissions (Linux)

When `ghr` starts a docker-mode runner container on Linux, the **`runner` user inside the container** (uid 1001) must be able to reach the Docker socket. The socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`) is typically owned by `root` and a `docker` group on the host (commonly GID 999 or 998). ghr handles this automatically by:

1. Querying the socket's owning GID on the host with `stat -c '%g' /var/run/docker.sock`.
2. Passing `--group-add <GID>` to `docker run` so the container's runner user becomes a member of that group.
3. Running a pre-flight `test -S` check before starting the container to catch missing or mis-configured sockets early.

On **macOS**, non-default Colima profiles also use alternate paths (e.g. `~/.colima/myprofile/docker.sock`). Set `docker_socket` in your host config to override (supported on Linux and macOS; not applicable on Windows):

content.text: Now run the full test suite one final time to confirm everything is clean:
All tests pass. Let me mark the last todo complete:

All done. Here's a summary of what was fixed:

**Root cause:** `startDocker()` only built the `-v socket:/var/run/docker.sock` bind-mount for `h.OS == "linux"`. macOS and Windows docker-mode runners started without any socket mount, so jobs inside the container couldn't reach the Docker daemon.

**Changes made:**

1. **`internal/runner/docker.go`** — Added a new `darwinDockerSockFlags()` helper (mount-only, no `--group-add`). Replaced the `if h.OS == "linux"` block with a `switch` covering all three platforms:
   - `linux`: preflight check + mount + `--group-add <GID>` (unchanged)
   - `darwin`: preflight check + mount only (no GID needed — VM socket is world-accessible)
   - `windows`: mount `/var/run/docker.sock` directly (Docker Desktop Linux containers mode exposes it via the Hyper-V/WSL2 VM)

2. **`internal/config/config.go`** — Allowed `docker_socket` on `darwin` hosts (previously rejected as Linux-only), enabling Colima non-default profile paths like `~/.colima/myprofile/docker.sock`.

3. **`internal/doctor/doctor.go`** — Renamed `checkLinuxDockerSocket` → `checkUnixDockerSocket` and extended `checkDocker` to call it for both `linux` and `darwin` hosts. Added macOS-specific hint text for the socket-not-found error.

4. **Tests** — Added `Test_darwinDockerSockFlags_*`, `Test_dockerStartCommand_darwinIncludesSocketMount`, `Test_dockerStartCommand_windowsIncludesSocketMount` in `docker_test.go`; added `TestValidate_dockerSocketOnDarwin` and `TestValidate_dockerSocketOnWindows_rejected` in `config_test.go`.

5. **Docs** — Updated `linux-on-macos.md` and `host-setup.md` to reflect that the socket is now mounted on macOS, `docker_socket` is supported on macOS, and the socket check in `ghr doctor` covers both Linux and macOS.

input.overview: The prior change fixed missing `/var/run/docker.sock` inside the runner container on Windows by bind-mounting it, but did not add `--group-add` like Linux. Your `permission denied` errors are the remaining gap: extend Windows docker-mode startup to append `--group-add <GID>` by reading the socket’s group from inside the Docker Linux engine via a disposable `docker run`.
input.plan:
# Windows: fix Docker socket permission denied for Linux runners

## What was already fixed

In `startDocker()` the `windows` branch now sets:

```362:366:internal/runner/docker.go
	case "windows":
		// Docker Desktop (Linux containers mode) maps /var/run/docker.sock via the Hyper-V/WSL2 VM;
		// bind-mount it so jobs can reach the Docker daemon the same way as on Linux/macOS.
		sockFlags = fmt.Sprintf("-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock ")
```

That addresses **missing socket** (`no such file or directory`). It does **not** address **EACCES** on the mounted socket.

## Why you still see `permission denied`

Same root cause as on bare Linux: the socket in the Docker Desktop **Linux engine** is typically `root:docker` with mode `660`. The official `actions-runner` image runs as user `runner` (uid **1001**), which is **not** in that group unless you pass **`--group-add <GID>`** to `docker run`.

On Linux, `dockerEngineSockFlags` already does this using host `stat -c '%g'` via `h.Run` (POSIX shell).

On Windows, remote automation uses **PowerShell** (`dockerRun` → `RunShell`); there is no reliable host-side Unix `stat` for `/var/run/docker.sock` because that path is resolved in the **Linux VM**, not on the Windows filesystem. So the previous implementation intentionally skipped GID detection and documented “no `--group-add` on Windows” — which is **incorrect** for Docker Desktop when the socket is group-restricted.

## Intended fix

1. **Add a Windows-specific helper** in `internal/runner/docker.go` (e.g. `dockerEngineSockFlagsWindows`) that:
   - Builds the mount: `-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`
   - Runs a **one-off container** through existing `dockerRun(h, ...)` to read the GID **inside the engine**, e.g.  
     `docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock <image> stat -c '%g' /var/run/docker.sock`
   - Use **`RunnerDockerImage`** (`ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest`) so no extra image is required after `setupDocker` (already pulled on Windows path).
   - Parse stdout for a numeric GID; if valid and not `0`, append `--group-add <gid> ` (same semantics as `dockerEngineSockFlags`).
   - If the probe fails, fall back to **mount-only** (current behavior) so we do not block startup in odd environments.

2. **Wire `startDocker` `case "windows"`** to call that helper instead of the static `sockFlags` string.

3. **Tests** in `internal/runner/docker_test.go`: unit-test the string-building logic with a small test seam if needed, or test a pure function that takes `(gid string) -> flags` plus integration-style test that the Windows branch’s command shape includes optional `--group-add`.

4. **Docs** in `docs/content/guides/linux-on-windows.md`: replace the sentence claiming `--group-add` is skipped on Windows with wording that ghr probes the socket GID via Docker and adds `--group-add` when needed (Docker Desktop Linux engine).

5. **Optional:** extend `ghr doctor` for Windows docker-mode running containers to `docker exec ... test -S /var/run/docker.sock` (mirroring `checkUnixDockerSocket`) — lower priority than fixing `startDocker`.

## Files to touch

- `internal/runner/docker.go` — Windows sock flags + GID probe via `dockerRun`
- `internal/runner/docker_test.go` — coverage for new helper / flags shape
- `docs/content/guides/linux-on-windows.md` — align with behavior

Add tests for Windows socket flag construction / --group-add when GID present
Update linux-on-windows.md to describe --group-add probing on Windows

input.old_string: **Docker socket permissions:** On Windows, Docker Desktop exposes the socket to all processes in the Hyper-V/WSL2 VM; there is no host `docker` group GID mismatch. The `--group-add` flag is only applied on Linux hosts and is skipped for Windows. No `docker_socket` override is needed or supported on Windows hosts.
input.new_string: **Docker socket permissions:** Inside Docker Desktop’s Linux engine, `/var/run/docker.sock` is often group-restricted (like on Linux). The official `actions-runner` image runs as user `runner` (uid 1001), so **ghr** probes the socket’s owning GID by running a short disposable `docker run` (using the same runner image) and, when it gets a non-zero numeric GID, passes **`--group-add`** on `docker run` for your runner container — the same fix as on bare Linux hosts, without needing Unix `stat` on the Windows side. If the probe fails, ghr still bind-mounts the socket (mount-only fallback). No `docker_socket` override is needed or supported on Windows hosts.

content.text: [REDACTED]

Here’s what was implemented:

### Cause
Linux runners on Windows only got `-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`. The socket in Docker Desktop’s Linux engine is usually `root:docker` mode `660`, so user `runner` (uid 1001) still hit **permission denied** without **`--group-add <GID>`**, same as on Linux. Windows can’t run host `stat` on that path, so the old code skipped GID handling.

### Code (`internal/runner/docker.go`)
- **`dockerWindowsEngineSockMount`** – constant for the bind mount.
- **`dockerWindowsSockGIDProbeCommand(image)`** – builds  
  `docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock --entrypoint sh <image> -c "stat -c '%g' /var/run/docker.sock"`  
  so GID is read **inside** the engine using **`RunnerDockerImage`** (already pulled in `setupDockerWindows`).
- **`appendGroupAddForDockerSockGID`** – trims output, accepts only non-empty numeric GIDs, skips `0`, appends `--group-add <gid> `.
- **`dockerEngineSockFlagsWindows(h)`** – runs the probe via **`dockerRun`** (PowerShell + `DOCKER_CONFIG` wrapper), with **`2>$null`**; on error uses **mount-only** fallback.
- **`startDocker`** `case "windows"` now uses **`dockerEngineSockFlagsWindows(h)`** instead of a fixed mount string.

### Tests (`internal/runner/docker_test.go`)
- **`Test_appendGroupAddForDockerSockGID`** – edge cases (empty, `0`, numeric, junk, `-1`).
- **`Test_dockerWindowsSockGIDProbeCommand_shape`** – expected probe fragments.
- **`Test_dockerStartCommand_windowsIncludesGroupAddWhenGIDKnown`** – full `dockerStartCommand` with `--group-add 999`.
- **`Test_dockerStartCommand_windowsIncludesSocketMount`** – uses **`dockerWindowsEngineSockMount`** (mount-only path).

Removed a test that called **`dockerEngineSockFlagsWindows`** on an unconnected host (it waited ~30s for SSH).

### Docs (`docs/content/guides/linux-on-windows.md`)
- Describes GID probing, **`--group-add`**, and mount-only fallback; still notes that **`docker_socket`** is not used on Windows.

`go test ./...` passes. After deploy, recreate existing Windows docker-mode runners (`ghr down <name> && ghr up <name>`) so they pick up the new flags.
